  • The configure tool's response redacts proxy credentials for security.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• CLI flags -- All available CLI flags
  • Config file -- Full config file reference
  • configure -- Runtime configuration tool reference
  • proxychains -- Using proxychains with yorishiro-proxy

docs.title: Auto-transform rules
docs.text: Auto-transform rules automatically modify matching requests and responses as they pass through the proxy. You can add, set, or remove headers and replace body content based on URL patterns, methods, and header conditions.

docs.title: How auto-transform works
docs.text:
When a request or response passes through the proxy:

  1. Each enabled auto-transform rule is evaluated in priority order (lower values first)
  2. If all conditions match, the rule's action is applied
  3. Multiple rules can apply to the same request or response

docs.title: Rule definition
docs.text:
Each auto-transform rule has the following fields:

Field Type Description id string Unique rule identifier enabled boolean Whether the rule is active priority integer Execution order (lower values applied first) direction string "request", "response", or "both" conditions object Matching criteria action object Transformation to apply

docs.title: Matching conditions
docs.text:
Field Type Description url_pattern string Regex matched against the full request URL methods array HTTP method whitelist (case-insensitive) header_match object Header name to regex mapping (AND logic)

All specified conditions must match (AND logic). An empty conditions object matches everything.

docs.title: Set a header
docs.text:
Replace a header value (or add it if not present):

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "inject-auth",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "url_pattern": "/api/.*"
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "set_header",
          "header": "Authorization",
          "value": "Bearer test-token-123"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Add a header
docs.text:
Add a header value (appends to existing values):

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "add-proxy-header",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 20,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {
          "type": "add_header",
          "header": "X-Proxied-By",
          "value": "yorishiro-proxy"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Remove a header
docs.text:
Remove a header from requests or responses:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "strip-csp",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "response",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {
          "type": "remove_header",
          "header": "Content-Security-Policy"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Replace body content
docs.text:
Search and replace text in the body using regex:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "replace-host",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {
          "type": "replace_body",
          "pattern": "production\\.example\\.com",
          "value": "staging.example.com"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Priority
docs.text:
Rules are applied in priority order, with lower values executed first. This lets you control the order of transformations:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "step-1",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {"type": "set_header", "header": "X-Step", "value": "1"}
      },
      {
        "id": "step-2",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 20,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {"type": "set_header", "header": "X-Step", "value": "2"}
      }
    ]
  }
}

In this example, step-1 runs first but step-2 overwrites the header value because it runs second.

docs.title: Enable and disable
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "disable": ["inject-auth"],
    "enable": ["strip-csp"]
  }
}

docs.title: Remove rules
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "remove": ["inject-auth"]
  }
}

docs.title: Replace all rules
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "operation": "replace",
  "auto_transform": {
    "rules": [
      {
        "id": "only-rule",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 0,
        "direction": "both",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {
          "type": "add_header",
          "header": "X-Proxy",
          "value": "yorishiro"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Inject authentication headers
docs.text:
Automatically add authentication headers to all API requests:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "auto-auth",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "url_pattern": "api\\.target\\.com/.*"
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "set_header",
          "header": "Authorization",
          "value": "Bearer your-api-token"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Strip security headers for testing
docs.text:
Remove security headers from responses to test client-side behavior:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "strip-hsts",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "response",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {"type": "remove_header", "header": "Strict-Transport-Security"}
      },
      {
        "id": "strip-csp",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 11,
        "direction": "response",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {"type": "remove_header", "header": "Content-Security-Policy"}
      },
      {
        "id": "strip-xfo",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 12,
        "direction": "response",
        "conditions": {},
        "action": {"type": "remove_header", "header": "X-Frame-Options"}
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Replace API endpoints
docs.text:
Redirect requests from production to staging:

// configure
{
  "auto_transform": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "redirect-to-staging",
        "enabled": true,
        "priority": 10,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "methods": ["POST", "PUT", "DELETE"]
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "replace_body",
          "pattern": "api\\.production\\.com",
          "value": "api.staging.com"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Configure tool reference -- Full configuration reference
  • Intercept -- Manual request/response interception

docs.title: Flow comparison
docs.text: The comparer lets you structurally compare two flows to identify differences in status codes, headers, body length, timing, and JSON content. It is designed as a triage tool for analyzing results from resend and fuzz operations.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The compare action takes two flow IDs and produces a structured diff of their responses. It compares:

  • Status code -- whether the HTTP status codes differ
  • Body length -- byte-level size difference
  • Headers -- added, removed, and changed headers
  • Timing -- response duration difference in milliseconds
  • Body content -- identical check, plus JSON key-level diff for JSON responses

docs.title: Basic comparison
docs.text:
// resend
{
  "action": "compare",
  "params": {
    "flow_id_a": "original-flow-123",
    "flow_id_b": "mutated-flow-456"
  }
}

docs.title: Response structure
docs.text: The compare result includes:

docs.title: Status code diff
docs.text:
{
  "status_code": {
    "a": 200,
    "b": 403,
    "changed": true
  }
}

docs.title: Body length diff
docs.text:
{
  "body_length": {
    "a": 1234,
    "b": 567,
    "delta": -667
  }
}

A positive delta means flow B has a larger body; negative means flow A is larger.

docs.title: Header diff
docs.text:
{
  "headers_added": ["X-New-Header"],
  "headers_removed": ["X-Old-Header"],
  "headers_changed": {
    "Content-Length": {
      "a": "1234",
      "b": "567"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Timing diff
docs.text:
{
  "timing_ms": {
    "a": 150,
    "b": 2500,
    "delta": 2350
  }
}

docs.title: Body diff
docs.text:
For all content types:

{
  "body": {
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "identical": false
  }
}

For JSON responses, the diff includes key-level analysis:

{
  "body": {
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "identical": false,
    "json_diff": {
      "keys_added": ["error_message"],
      "keys_removed": ["data"],
      "keys_changed": ["status", "timestamp"]
    }
  }
}

JSON key-level diff

The JSON diff operates on top-level keys only. It identifies which keys were added, removed, or changed between the two responses.

docs.title: Resend triage
docs.text:
After resending a request with mutations, compare the original and modified responses to identify what changed:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "original-123",
    "body_patches": [{"json_path": "$.role", "value": "admin"}]
  }
}

Then compare:

// resend
{
  "action": "compare",
  "params": {
    "flow_id_a": "original-123",
    "flow_id_b": "new-flow-id-from-resend"
  }
}

docs.title: Fuzz result analysis
docs.text:
Compare fuzz results against a baseline to find interesting responses:

// resend
{
  "action": "compare",
  "params": {
    "flow_id_a": "baseline-flow",
    "flow_id_b": "fuzz-result-flow"
  }
}

Look for:

  • Status code changes -- a 200 to 500 transition may indicate a vulnerability
  • Body length changes -- significant size differences may reveal information disclosure
  • Timing anomalies -- large timing deltas may indicate blind injection points
  • JSON key changes -- new keys may reveal additional data exposure

docs.title: Before/after testing
docs.text:
Compare the same endpoint before and after a code change:

// resend
{
  "action": "compare",
  "params": {
    "flow_id_a": "before-patch-flow",
    "flow_id_b": "after-patch-flow"
  }
}

docs.title: Getting full details
docs.text:
The comparer is designed as a triage tool. For full response bodies and detailed analysis, use the query tool:

// query
{
  "action": "messages",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "flow-to-inspect"
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Resend tool reference -- Compare action parameter reference
  • Resender -- Resend with mutations before comparing
  • Fuzzer -- Generate multiple flows for comparison

docs.title: Flow export and import
docs.text: You can export recorded flows to share with other tools or team members, and import flows from previous sessions. yorishiro-proxy supports three export formats: JSONL, HAR 1.2, and cURL commands.

docs.title: JSONL format
docs.text:
JSONL (JSON Lines) is the native export format. Each line is a complete JSON object containing a flow and its messages. This format supports all protocols and flow types, including raw TCP and gRPC.

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl",
    "output_path": "/tmp/flows.jsonl"
  }
}

When output_path is omitted, small exports (up to 100 flows) are returned inline in the response:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: HAR 1.2 format
docs.text:
HAR (HTTP Archive) 1.2 is a standard format compatible with browser DevTools, Burp Suite, and OWASP ZAP. HAR export always requires a file path:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "har",
    "output_path": "/tmp/flows.har"
  }
}

HAR limitations

HAR is an HTTP-specific format. Raw TCP and gRPC flows are excluded from HAR exports. WebSocket flows include a _webSocketMessages custom field. Binary bodies are Base64-encoded with content.encoding: "base64".

docs.title: cURL command format
docs.text:
Export individual flows as cURL commands using the query tool:

// query
{
  "action": "curl",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123"
  }
}

docs.title: Filtering exports
docs.text:
Apply filters to export a subset of flows:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl",
    "filter": {
      "protocol": "HTTPS",
      "url_pattern": "/api/",
      "time_after": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z",
      "time_before": "2026-02-28T23:59:59Z"
    },
    "output_path": "/tmp/api-flows.jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: Filter fields
docs.text: Field Type Description protocol string Filter by protocol (e.g. "HTTPS", "HTTP/1.x", "WebSocket") url_pattern string Filter by URL substring time_after string Include flows after this time (RFC3339 format) time_before string Include flows before this time (RFC3339 format)

docs.title: Metadata-only export
docs.text:
Export flow metadata without message bodies for a lightweight overview:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl",
    "include_bodies": false,
    "output_path": "/tmp/flows-meta.jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: Importing flows
docs.text:
Import flows from a JSONL file. Each line must be a valid export record with version "1":

// manage
{
  "action": "import_flows",
  "params": {
    "input_path": "/tmp/flows.jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: Conflict handling
docs.text:
When importing flows with IDs that already exist in the store, use on_conflict to control behavior:

Policy Description skip Skip flows with existing IDs (default) replace Delete existing flows and re-import 
// manage
{
  "action": "import_flows",
  "params": {
    "input_path": "/tmp/flows.jsonl",
    "on_conflict": "replace"
  }
}

docs.title: Import result
docs.text:
The import action returns:

Field Description imported Number of flows successfully imported skipped Number of flows skipped due to conflicts errors Number of flows that failed to import source Absolute path of the import file error_details Details of any import errors

docs.title: Export for external analysis
docs.text:
Export HTTPS flows to HAR for analysis in Burp Suite:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "har",
    "filter": {"protocol": "HTTPS"},
    "output_path": "/tmp/analysis.har"
  }
}

docs.title: Session backup
docs.text:
Export all flows from a testing session:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl",
    "output_path": "/tmp/session-backup.jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: Restore a session
docs.text:
Import a previous session's flows:

// manage
{
  "action": "import_flows",
  "params": {
    "input_path": "/tmp/session-backup.jsonl",
    "on_conflict": "skip"
  }
}

docs.title: Time-scoped export
docs.text:
Export flows from a specific time window:

// manage
{
  "action": "export_flows",
  "params": {
    "format": "jsonl",
    "filter": {
      "time_after": "2026-03-14T09:00:00Z",
      "time_before": "2026-03-14T17:00:00Z"
    },
    "output_path": "/tmp/today.jsonl"
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Manage tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Flows -- Flow data model
  • Query tool reference -- cURL export via query

docs.title: Fuzzer
docs.text: The fuzzer executes automated testing campaigns by injecting payloads into recorded proxy requests. You define injection positions, payload sets, and execution parameters, then the fuzzer runs asynchronously and records all results as new flows.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Select a recorded flow as the template
  2. Define positions where payloads should be injected
  3. Configure payload sets with the values to inject
  4. Start the fuzz job -- it runs asynchronously and returns a fuzz_id
  5. Query results with the query tool using fuzz_results

docs.title: Position definitions
docs.text:
Positions specify where payloads are injected in the request. Each position requires:

  • id -- unique identifier (e.g. "pos-0")
  • location -- where to inject: header, path, query, body_regex, body_json, cookie
  • payload_set -- which payload set to use

docs.title: Header position
docs.text:
Replace or add a header value:

// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {
        "id": "pos-0",
        "location": "header",
        "name": "Authorization",
        "mode": "replace",
        "match": "Bearer (.*)",
        "payload_set": "tokens"
      }
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "tokens": {
        "type": "wordlist",
        "values": ["token1", "token2", "admin-token"]
      }
    }
  }
}

The match field uses regex for partial replacement. When a capture group is specified, only the group is replaced.

docs.title: Query position
docs.text:
Inject into a URL query parameter:

{
  "id": "pos-0",
  "location": "query",
  "name": "search",
  "payload_set": "xss"
}

docs.title: Body JSON position
docs.text:
Inject into a JSON body field using JSON path:

{
  "id": "pos-0",
  "location": "body_json",
  "json_path": "$.password",
  "payload_set": "passwords"
}

docs.title: Body regex position
docs.text:
Inject by matching a regex pattern in the body:

{
  "id": "pos-0",
  "location": "body_regex",
  "match": "csrf_token=([^&]+)",
  "payload_set": "tokens"
}

docs.title: Cookie position
docs.text:
Inject into a specific cookie:

{
  "id": "pos-0",
  "location": "cookie",
  "name": "session",
  "payload_set": "session_ids"
}

docs.title: Position modes
docs.text: Mode Description replace Replace the current value (default) add Add a new entry (for headers, query params) remove Remove the entry entirely (no payload_set needed)

docs.title: Payload sets
docs.text: Payload sets define the values to inject at each position.

docs.title: Wordlist
docs.text:
Inline list of payload strings:

{
  "type": "wordlist",
  "values": ["<script>alert(1)</script>", "' OR 1=1 --", "{{7*7}}"]
}

docs.title: File
docs.text:
Load payloads from a file under ~/.yorishiro-proxy/wordlists/:

{
  "type": "file",
  "path": "sqli/generic.txt"
}

docs.title: Range
docs.text:
Generate integer payloads from start to end:

{
  "type": "range",
  "start": 1,
  "end": 100,
  "step": 1
}

docs.title: Sequence
docs.text:
Generate formatted sequential strings:

{
  "type": "sequence",
  "start": 1,
  "end": 50,
  "format": "user%04d"
}

This produces user0001, user0002, ..., user0050.

docs.title: Charset
docs.text:
Generate all combinations of a character set with a given length:

{
  "type": "charset",
  "charset": "0123456789",
  "length": 4
}

This generates all 4-digit PINs: 0000, 0001, ..., 9999.

docs.title: Case variation
docs.text:
Generate all case variations of a string:

{
  "type": "case_variation",
  "input": "Admin"
}

This produces admin, Admin, ADMIN, aDmin, etc.

docs.title: Null byte injection
docs.text:
Generate null byte injection variants of a string:

{
  "type": "null_byte_injection",
  "input": "test.php"
}

docs.title: Encoding chains
docs.text:
Each payload set can include an encoding array that applies codec transformations to every payload before injection:

{
  "type": "wordlist",
  "values": ["<script>alert(1)</script>", "<img onerror=alert(1)>"],
  "encoding": ["url_encode_query"]
}

Codecs are applied in pipeline order. For example, ["url_encode_query", "base64"] first URL-encodes the payload, then Base64-encodes the result.

Available codecs: base64, base64url, url_encode_query, url_encode_path, url_encode_full, double_url_encode, hex, html_entity, html_escape, unicode_escape, md5, sha256, lower, upper.

Maximum chain length is 10 codecs.

docs.title: Sequential mode
docs.text:
Tests one position at a time while keeping other positions at their original values. Each position cycles through all payloads from its payload set independently.

Total requests = sum of all payload set sizes.

// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "username", "payload_set": "users"},
      {"id": "pos-1", "location": "body_json", "json_path": "$.password", "payload_set": "passwords"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "users": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["admin", "root"]},
      "passwords": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["pass1", "pass2", "pass3"]}
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Parallel mode
docs.text:
Applies payloads to all positions simultaneously using a zip strategy. Payload sets are consumed in lockstep.

Total requests = max payload set size.

// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "parallel",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "username", "payload_set": "users"},
      {"id": "pos-1", "location": "body_json", "json_path": "$.password", "payload_set": "passwords"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "users": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["admin", "root", "user"]},
      "passwords": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["pass1", "pass2", "pass3"]}
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Execution parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description concurrency integer 1 Number of concurrent workers rate_limit_rps number 0 (unlimited) Requests per second limit delay_ms integer 0 Fixed delay between requests in ms timeout_ms integer 10000 Per-request timeout in ms max_retries integer 0 Retry count per failed request

docs.title: Async execution and job management
docs.text: Fuzz jobs run asynchronously. The fuzz action returns a fuzz_id immediately.

docs.title: Starting a job
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "q", "payload_set": "xss"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "xss": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["<script>", "{{7*7}}", "' OR 1=1"]}
    },
    "concurrency": 5,
    "rate_limit_rps": 10,
    "tag": "xss-scan"
  }
}

docs.title: Pausing a job
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz_pause",
  "params": {"fuzz_id": "fuzz-abc-123"}
}

Workers stop after completing their current request.

docs.title: Resuming a job
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz_resume",
  "params": {"fuzz_id": "fuzz-abc-123"}
}

docs.title: Canceling a job
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz_cancel",
  "params": {"fuzz_id": "fuzz-abc-123"}
}

docs.title: Stop conditions
docs.text:
Define automatic stop conditions to end a fuzz job early:

// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "id", "payload_set": "ids"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "ids": {"type": "range", "start": 1, "end": 10000}
    },
    "stop_on": {
      "status_codes": [500, 503],
      "error_count": 10,
      "latency_threshold_ms": 5000,
      "latency_baseline_multiplier": 3.0,
      "latency_window": 10
    }
  }
}
Condition Description status_codes Stop when any of these HTTP status codes is received error_count Stop when cumulative error count reaches this value latency_threshold_ms Stop when sliding window median latency exceeds this value latency_baseline_multiplier Stop when current median exceeds baseline median times this multiplier latency_window Sliding window size for latency detection (default: 10)

docs.title: Querying results
docs.text:
Use the query tool with fuzz_results to check progress and retrieve results:

// query
{
  "action": "fuzz_results",
  "params": {
    "fuzz_id": "fuzz-abc-123"
  }
}

Results include status codes, response sizes, timing, and the payloads used for each request.

docs.title: Hooks integration
docs.text:
The fuzzer supports the same pre/post hook system as the resender. Use hooks to refresh authentication tokens between fuzz iterations:

// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "body_json", "json_path": "$.password", "payload_set": "passwords"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "passwords": {"type": "wordlist", "values": ["test1", "test2"]}
    },
    "hooks": {
      "pre_send": {
        "macro": "refresh-auth",
        "run_interval": "every_n",
        "n": 10
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: XSS scanning
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "search-flow",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "q", "payload_set": "xss"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "xss": {
        "type": "wordlist",
        "values": [
          "<script>alert(1)</script>",
          "<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>",
          "{{7*7}}",
          "${7*7}"
        ],
        "encoding": ["url_encode_query"]
      }
    },
    "tag": "xss-scan"
  }
}

docs.title: Brute force PIN
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "pin-verify-flow",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "body_json", "json_path": "$.pin", "payload_set": "pins"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "pins": {
        "type": "charset",
        "charset": "0123456789",
        "length": 4
      }
    },
    "concurrency": 3,
    "rate_limit_rps": 5,
    "stop_on": {"status_codes": [200]}
  }
}

docs.title: IDOR enumeration
docs.text:
// fuzz
{
  "action": "fuzz",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "user-profile-flow",
    "attack_type": "sequential",
    "positions": [
      {"id": "pos-0", "location": "query", "name": "user_id", "payload_set": "ids"}
    ],
    "payload_sets": {
      "ids": {"type": "range", "start": 1, "end": 1000}
    },
    "tag": "idor-scan"
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Fuzz tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Resender -- Single request resend with mutations
  • Comparer -- Compare fuzz results against baselines
  • Built-in codecs -- Available encoding codecs

docs.title: Intercept
docs.text: The intercept feature lets you pause HTTP requests and responses in transit, inspect them, and decide whether to release, modify, or drop them. This gives you real-time control over traffic flowing through the proxy.

docs.title: How interception works
docs.text:
1. Define intercept rules that match specific requests or responses
  2. When a matching request or response passes through the proxy, it is held in the intercept queue
  3. The AI agent (or WebUI user) reviews the queued item
  4. The item is released as-is, modified and forwarded, or dropped

docs.title: Configuring intercept rules
docs.text:
Intercept rules define which requests and responses are captured. You configure rules using the configure tool:

// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "api-requests",
        "enabled": true,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "host_pattern": "api\\.target\\.com",
          "path_pattern": "/api/.*",
          "methods": ["POST", "PUT", "DELETE"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Rule fields
docs.text: Field Type Description id string Unique rule identifier enabled boolean Whether the rule is active direction string "request", "response", or "both" conditions object Matching criteria

docs.title: Condition fields
docs.text:
Field Type Description host_pattern string Regex matched against the hostname (port excluded) path_pattern string Regex matched against the URL path methods array HTTP method whitelist (case-insensitive) header_match object Header name to regex mapping (AND logic)

All specified conditions must match for a rule to trigger (AND logic).

docs.title: Direction
docs.text:
• request -- intercepts the request before it reaches the upstream server
  • response -- intercepts the response before it reaches the client
  • both -- intercepts both the request and the response

Intercepted items include a phase field ("request" or "response") so you know which direction was captured.

docs.title: Viewing queued items
docs.text:
Use the query tool to list items waiting in the intercept queue:

// query
{
  "action": "intercept_queue"
}

Each queued item has an intercept_id that you use for subsequent actions.

docs.title: Release
docs.text:
Forward the intercepted item as-is, without modifications:

// intercept
{
  "action": "release",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-abc-123"
  }
}

docs.title: Modify and forward
docs.text:
Modify the intercepted item before forwarding. The available mutation parameters depend on the phase.

Request phase mutations:

// intercept
{
  "action": "modify_and_forward",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-abc-123",
    "override_method": "POST",
    "override_url": "https://api.target.com/v2/users",
    "override_headers": {"Authorization": "Bearer injected-token"},
    "add_headers": {"X-Custom": "value"},
    "remove_headers": ["X-Debug"],
    "override_body": "{\"role\":\"admin\"}"
  }
}

Response phase mutations:

// intercept
{
  "action": "modify_and_forward",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-resp-456",
    "override_status": 200,
    "override_response_headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
    "add_response_headers": {"X-Modified": "true"},
    "remove_response_headers": ["X-Server-Info"],
    "override_response_body": "{\"success\": true}"
  }
}

docs.title: Drop
docs.text:
Discard the intercepted item. For requests, this returns a 502 Bad Gateway response to the client:

// intercept
{
  "action": "drop",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-abc-123"
  }
}

docs.title: Request phase parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description override_method string Override the HTTP method override_url string Override the target URL override_headers object Header overrides (key-value pairs) add_headers object Headers to add remove_headers array Header names to remove override_body string Override the request body

docs.title: Response phase parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description override_status integer Override the HTTP status code override_response_headers object Response header overrides add_response_headers object Response headers to add remove_response_headers array Response header names to remove override_response_body string Override the response body

docs.title: Queue configuration
docs.text:
Configure the intercept queue behavior using the configure tool:

// configure
{
  "intercept_queue": {
    "timeout_ms": 300000,
    "timeout_behavior": "auto_release"
  }
}
Parameter Type Default Description timeout_ms integer 300000 (5 min) Timeout for blocked requests (minimum 1000 ms) timeout_behavior string auto_release What happens on timeout: auto_release or auto_drop

When a blocked request times out:

  • auto_release -- the request is forwarded as-is (default)
  • auto_drop -- the request is dropped with a 502 response

docs.title: Enable and disable rules
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "disable": ["api-requests"],
    "enable": ["other-rule"]
  }
}

docs.title: Remove rules
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "remove": ["api-requests"]
  }
}

docs.title: Replace all rules
docs.text:
// configure
{
  "operation": "replace",
  "intercept_rules": {
    "rules": [
      {
        "id": "new-rule",
        "enabled": true,
        "direction": "both",
        "conditions": {
          "path_pattern": "/api/.*"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Raw bytes mode
docs.text:
By default, the intercept tool operates in structured mode -- it parses and serializes requests at the HTTP (L7) layer. This means modifications go through the standard HTTP library, which may normalize headers, adjust Content-Length, and apply transfer encoding.

Raw bytes mode bypasses this entirely. When you set "mode": "raw", the proxy sends bytes directly on the wire without any HTTP library processing. This gives you full control over every byte of the request or response.

docs.title: Structured vs raw mode
docs.text: Aspect Structured mode (default) Raw mode Layer L7 (HTTP semantics) L4 (raw bytes on the wire) Modifications override_method, override_headers, etc. raw_override_base64 (entire payload) Normalization HTTP library may adjust headers, Content-Length, encoding None -- bytes are forwarded as-is Auto-transform Applied (e.g., gzip decompression for display) Not applied Use case Standard request/response editing Protocol-level anomaly testing

docs.title: HTTP/1.x raw forwarding
docs.text:
In raw mode for HTTP/1.x, the proxy writes your raw bytes directly to a TCP (or TLS) connection to the upstream server, completely bypassing net/http.Transport. The raw response from upstream is read back and forwarded to the client as-is.

This enables testing scenarios where HTTP library normalization would otherwise mask the issue:

  • HTTP Request Smuggling -- craft requests with conflicting Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers (CL/TE, TE/CL)
  • Header injection -- send malformed headers that a standard HTTP library would reject or rewrite
  • Protocol downgrade testing -- send intentionally non-conformant HTTP to observe server behavior

docs.title: HTTP/2 raw forwarding
docs.text:
For HTTP/2, raw mode operates on individual frames rather than the complete connection stream. When you provide raw bytes, the proxy:

  1. Parses the 9-byte frame headers to locate stream ID fields
  2. Rewrites stream IDs to match the upstream connection's allocated stream (connection-level frames with stream ID 0 are not rewritten)
  3. Forwards the frames as-is, preserving all other bytes including flags, padding, and payload

This means you can craft custom HEADERS, DATA, or other frame types while the proxy handles the stream multiplexing automatically.

docs.title: Using raw mode
docs.text:
Release with raw bytes (forward original wire bytes without HTTP normalization):

// intercept
{
  "action": "release",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-abc-123",
    "mode": "raw"
  }
}

Modify and forward with raw bytes (replace the entire payload):

// intercept
{
  "action": "modify_and_forward",
  "params": {
    "intercept_id": "int-abc-123",
    "mode": "raw",
    "raw_override_base64": "R0VUIC8gSFRUUC8xLjENCkhvc3Q6IGV4YW1wbGUuY29tDQoNCg=="
  }
}

The raw_override_base64 value is the Base64 encoding of the complete raw bytes you want sent on the wire. For HTTP/1.x, this is the full request including the request line, headers, and body. For HTTP/2, this is one or more serialized frames.

Note

Raw mode requires raw bytes to be available for the intercepted item. The response includes raw_bytes_available: true when this is the case.

docs.title: WebUI integration
docs.text: The intercept queue is also accessible through the Web UI, where you can visually inspect and act on queued items. The WebUI provides a hex dump editor and text editor for raw bytes editing. See WebUI Intercept for details.

docs.title: Inspect login requests
docs.text:
Intercept login requests to test different credential combinations in real time:

// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "login-intercept",
        "enabled": true,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "path_pattern": "/login",
          "methods": ["POST"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Modify API responses
docs.text:
Intercept responses to test how the client handles different server behaviors:

// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "api-responses",
        "enabled": true,
        "direction": "response",
        "conditions": {
          "host_pattern": "api\\.target\\.com",
          "header_match": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Intercept by header
docs.text:
Intercept only requests with specific headers:

// configure
{
  "intercept_rules": {
    "add": [
      {
        "id": "auth-requests",
        "enabled": true,
        "direction": "request",
        "conditions": {
          "header_match": {"Authorization": "Bearer.*"}
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Intercept tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Configure tool reference -- Rule configuration reference
  • WebUI Intercept -- WebUI intercept guide

docs.title: Macros
docs.text: Macros let you define multi-step workflows that execute a sequence of HTTP requests with variable extraction, template expansion, and conditional logic. They are essential for testing authenticated flows, chained API operations, and any scenario requiring state between requests.

docs.title: Core concepts
docs.text:
A macro consists of:

  • Steps -- ordered HTTP requests based on recorded flows
  • KV Store -- a key-value store for passing data between steps via §variable§ templates
  • Extraction rules -- rules to extract values from responses into the KV Store
  • Guards -- conditions that control whether a step executes

docs.title: Defining a macro
docs.text:
Use the define_macro action to save a macro definition. If a macro with the same name exists, it is updated (upsert):

// macro
{
  "action": "define_macro",
  "params": {
    "name": "auth-flow",
    "description": "Login and get CSRF token",
    "steps": [
      {
        "id": "login",
        "flow_id": "recorded-login-flow",
        "override_body": "username=admin&password=§password§",
        "extract": [
          {
            "name": "session_cookie",
            "from": "response",
            "source": "header",
            "header_name": "Set-Cookie",
            "regex": "PHPSESSID=([^;]+)",
            "group": 1
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "get-csrf",
        "flow_id": "recorded-csrf-flow",
        "override_headers": {"Cookie": "PHPSESSID=§session_cookie§"},
        "extract": [
          {
            "name": "csrf_token",
            "from": "response",
            "source": "body",
            "regex": "name=\"csrf\" value=\"([^\"]+)\"",
            "group": 1
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "initial_vars": {"password": "admin123"}
  }
}

docs.title: Step definition
docs.text:
Each step specifies a recorded flow as a template and optional mutations:

Field Type Description id string Unique step identifier (required) flow_id string Recorded flow to use as template (required) override_method string Override HTTP method override_url string Override request URL (supports §variable§ templates) override_headers object Header overrides (supports templates) override_body string Override request body (supports templates) on_error string Error handling: "abort" (default), "skip", "retry" retry_count integer Retry count when on_error is "retry" (default: 3) retry_delay_ms integer Delay between retries in ms (default: 1000) timeout_ms integer Step timeout in ms (default: 60000) extract array Value extraction rules when object Guard condition for conditional execution

docs.title: Variable extraction
docs.text: Extraction rules pull values from responses and store them in the KV Store for use in subsequent steps.

docs.title: Extraction sources
docs.text: Source Description header Extract from a response header (requires header_name) body Extract from the response body using regex body_json Extract from a JSON response using json_path status Extract the HTTP status code url Extract from the response URL (after redirects)

docs.title: Extraction from headers
docs.text:
{
  "name": "session_cookie",
  "from": "response",
  "source": "header",
  "header_name": "Set-Cookie",
  "regex": "PHPSESSID=([^;]+)",
  "group": 1
}

docs.title: Extraction from body with regex
docs.text:
{
  "name": "csrf_token",
  "from": "response",
  "source": "body",
  "regex": "name=\"csrf\" value=\"([^\"]+)\"",
  "group": 1
}

docs.title: Extraction from JSON body
docs.text:
{
  "name": "access_token",
  "from": "response",
  "source": "body_json",
  "json_path": "$.data.access_token"
}

docs.title: Extraction from status code
docs.text:
{
  "name": "login_status",
  "from": "response",
  "source": "status"
}

docs.title: Extraction options
docs.text: Field Type Description name string Variable name to store in KV Store (required) from string "request" or "response" (required) source string Extraction source (required) header_name string Header name (for header source) regex string Regex pattern (for header and body sources) group integer Capture group index (default: 0 = full match) json_path string JSON path (for body_json source) default string Default value if extraction fails required boolean Fail the step if extraction fails

docs.title: Template expansion
docs.text:
Use §variable§ syntax in override_url, override_headers, and override_body to reference KV Store values:

{
  "id": "api-call",
  "flow_id": "api-flow",
  "override_url": "https://api.target.com/v1/users/§user_id§",
  "override_headers": {
    "Authorization": "Bearer §access_token§",
    "Cookie": "session=§session_cookie§"
  },
  "override_body": "{\"csrf\": \"§csrf_token§\"}"
}

Variables are expanded at execution time, after any preceding step's extractions have populated the KV Store.

docs.title: Guards (conditional execution)
docs.text:
Guards control whether a step executes based on conditions from a previous step's result:

{
  "id": "admin-action",
  "flow_id": "admin-flow",
  "when": {
    "step": "login",
    "status_code": 200,
    "body_match": "\"role\":\"admin\""
  }
}

docs.title: Guard fields
docs.text: Field Type Description step string Step ID to evaluate (must be a previous step) status_code integer Require exact status code match status_code_range [int, int] Require status code within range (inclusive) header_match object Header name-to-regex mapping (AND logic) body_match string Regex pattern to match in response body extracted_var string Require this KV Store variable to exist and be non-empty negate boolean Invert the guard condition

docs.title: Negated guard example
docs.text:
Skip a step if the login returned a 403:

{
  "id": "dashboard",
  "flow_id": "dashboard-flow",
  "when": {
    "step": "login",
    "status_code": 403,
    "negate": true
  }
}

docs.title: Running a macro
docs.text:
Execute a stored macro with optional runtime variable overrides:

// macro
{
  "action": "run_macro",
  "params": {
    "name": "auth-flow",
    "vars": {"password": "different-password"}
  }
}

Runtime vars take precedence over initial_vars defined in the macro.

The result includes:

  • status -- "completed", "error", or "timeout"
  • steps_executed -- number of steps that ran
  • kv_store -- final KV Store contents
  • step_results -- per-step status codes, durations, and errors

docs.title: Hook integration
docs.text: Macros power the hook system used by Resender and Fuzzer. Hooks execute macros at specific points during resend or fuzz operations.

docs.title: Pre-send hooks
docs.text:
Execute a macro before sending the main request. The macro's KV Store values are used for template expansion in the main request parameters:

{
  "hooks": {
    "pre_send": {
      "macro": "refresh-auth",
      "run_interval": "always"
    }
  }
}

Run intervals for pre_send:

Interval Description always Run before every request (default) once Run only before the first request every_n Run every N requests (requires n parameter) on_error Run when the previous request had an error (4xx/5xx)

docs.title: Post-receive hooks
docs.text:
Execute a macro after receiving the main response:

{
  "hooks": {
    "post_receive": {
      "macro": "log-response",
      "run_interval": "on_status",
      "status_codes": [401, 403],
      "pass_response": true
    }
  }
}

When pass_response is true, the macro receives __response_status and __response_body as variables.

Run intervals for post_receive:

Interval Description always Run after every response (default) on_status Run when the response status matches status_codes on_match Run when the response body matches match_pattern regex

docs.title: Retry policy
docs.text:
Steps can be configured with automatic retry on failure:

{
  "id": "flaky-api",
  "flow_id": "api-flow",
  "on_error": "retry",
  "retry_count": 3,
  "retry_delay_ms": 2000,
  "timeout_ms": 10000
}
Error handling Description abort Stop the macro on error (default) skip Skip the failed step and continue retry Retry the step up to retry_count times

docs.title: Deleting a macro
docs.text:
// macro
{
  "action": "delete_macro",
  "params": {"name": "auth-flow"}
}

docs.title: Practical example: login, token, API access
docs.text:
This example demonstrates a complete authentication flow:

// macro
{
  "action": "define_macro",
  "params": {
    "name": "full-auth-flow",
    "description": "Login, extract token, access protected API",
    "steps": [
      {
        "id": "login",
        "flow_id": "login-flow-id",
        "override_body": "{\"username\":\"§username§\",\"password\":\"§password§\"}",
        "extract": [
          {
            "name": "access_token",
            "from": "response",
            "source": "body_json",
            "json_path": "$.data.access_token",
            "required": true
          },
          {
            "name": "refresh_token",
            "from": "response",
            "source": "body_json",
            "json_path": "$.data.refresh_token"
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "get-profile",
        "flow_id": "profile-flow-id",
        "override_headers": {
          "Authorization": "Bearer §access_token§"
        },
        "when": {
          "step": "login",
          "status_code_range": [200, 299]
        },
        "extract": [
          {
            "name": "user_id",
            "from": "response",
            "source": "body_json",
            "json_path": "$.data.id"
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "admin-action",
        "flow_id": "admin-flow-id",
        "override_url": "https://api.target.com/admin/users/§user_id§",
        "override_headers": {
          "Authorization": "Bearer §access_token§"
        },
        "when": {
          "step": "get-profile",
          "extracted_var": "user_id"
        }
      }
    ],
    "initial_vars": {
      "username": "admin",
      "password": "admin123"
    },
    "macro_timeout_ms": 60000
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Macro tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Resender -- Hook integration for single resends
  • Fuzzer -- Hook integration for fuzz campaigns

docs.title: Rate limits and diagnostic budgets
docs.text: Rate limits and diagnostic budgets control how aggressively the proxy sends requests. Rate limits cap the requests per second, while budgets cap the total number of requests or session duration. Both use the two-layer architecture (Policy + Agent) for safe operation.

docs.title: Global RPS
docs.text:
Limit the total number of requests per second across all hosts:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 10
  }
}

docs.title: Per-host RPS
docs.text:
Limit requests per second to each individual host:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 5
  }
}

docs.title: Combined limits
docs.text:
Set both global and per-host limits:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 50,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 10
  }
}

docs.title: Clearing limits
docs.text:
Set a limit to 0 to remove it. Omitted fields also reset to 0 (no limit) because set_rate_limits uses full-replace semantics:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 0,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Rate limit enforcement
docs.text: Requests that exceed rate limits receive a 429 Too Many Requests response with an X-Blocked-By: rate_limit header.

docs.title: Diagnostic budgets
docs.text: Budgets limit the total scope of a testing session by capping either the number of requests or the duration.

docs.title: Request budget
docs.text:
Limit the total number of requests:

// security
{
  "action": "set_budget",
  "params": {
    "max_total_requests": 1000
  }
}

docs.title: Duration budget
docs.text:
Limit the session duration using Go duration strings:

// security
{
  "action": "set_budget",
  "params": {
    "max_duration": "30m"
  }
}

Supported duration formats: "30m", "1h", "2h30m", "0s" (no limit).

docs.title: Combined budgets
docs.text:
// security
{
  "action": "set_budget",
  "params": {
    "max_total_requests": 5000,
    "max_duration": "2h"
  }
}

docs.title: Budget exhaustion
docs.text:
When a budget is exhausted, the proxy automatically stops accepting new requests. The stop_reason field in the budget status indicates why:

  • "max_total_requests exceeded"
  • "max_duration exceeded"

docs.title: Two-layer architecture
docs.text: Rate limits and budgets use the same two-layer design as Target scope:

docs.title: Policy layer
docs.text: Set at startup from the config file. Defines the upper bounds that the agent cannot exceed. Immutable at runtime.

docs.title: Agent layer
docs.text: Controlled by the AI agent via the security tool. Can set limits that are equal to or stricter than Policy limits, but never more permissive.

docs.title: Effective values
docs.text: The effective limit is always the stricter of the two layers. For example, if the Policy sets 50 RPS and the Agent sets 10 RPS, the effective limit is 10 RPS.

docs.title: Rate limits
docs.text:
// security
{
  "action": "get_rate_limits"
}

Response:

{
  "policy": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 50,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 20
  },
  "agent": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 10,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 5
  },
  "effective": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 10,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 5
  }
}

docs.title: Budget status
docs.text:
// security
{
  "action": "get_budget"
}

Response:

{
  "policy": {
    "max_total_requests": 5000,
    "max_duration": "2h0m0s"
  },
  "agent": {
    "max_total_requests": 1000,
    "max_duration": "30m0s"
  },
  "effective": {
    "max_total_requests": 1000,
    "max_duration": "30m0s"
  },
  "request_count": 142,
  "stop_reason": ""
}

docs.title: Budget via configure tool
docs.text:
You can also set budgets using the configure tool, which uses merge semantics by default (only update provided fields, keep others unchanged):

// configure
{
  "budget": {
    "max_total_requests": 1000,
    "max_duration": "30m"
  }
}

This differs from the security tool's set_budget action, which uses full-replace semantics (omitted fields reset to 0).

docs.title: Config file setup
docs.text:
Set Policy layer limits in the config file:

{
  "rate_limits": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 50,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 20
  },
  "budget": {
    "max_total_requests": 10000,
    "max_duration": "4h"
  }
}

docs.title: Conservative scanning
docs.text:
For initial reconnaissance with minimal impact:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 5,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 2
  }
}

// security
{
  "action": "set_budget",
  "params": {
    "max_total_requests": 500,
    "max_duration": "15m"
  }
}

docs.title: Intensive fuzzing
docs.text:
For targeted fuzzing within operator-approved limits:

// security
{
  "action": "set_rate_limits",
  "params": {
    "max_requests_per_second": 100,
    "max_requests_per_host_per_second": 50
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Security tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Configure tool reference -- Budget via configure
  • Target scope -- Host-level access control
  • SafetyFilter -- Content-level filtering
  • Config file -- Policy layer configuration

docs.title: Resender
docs.text: The resender lets you replay recorded proxy requests with optional mutations. You can override the method, URL, headers, and body, apply JSON patches or regex replacements, preview changes with dry-run, and resend raw bytes for protocol-level testing.

docs.title: Actions overview
docs.text: Action Description resend Resend an HTTP/HTTP2/WebSocket request with optional mutations resend_raw Resend raw bytes over TCP/TLS with byte-level patches tcp_replay Replay a Raw TCP flow by sending all send messages sequentially compare Compare two flows structurally (see Comparer)

docs.title: Resending with overrides
docs.text: The resend action replays a recorded flow and lets you override any part of the request. The original flow is not modified; a new flow is created with the result.

docs.title: Method override
docs.text:
Change the HTTP method of the request:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_method": "PUT"
  }
}

docs.title: URL override
docs.text:
Redirect the request to a different URL. The URL must include both the scheme and host:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_url": "https://staging.target.com/api/v2/users"
  }
}

docs.title: Header mutations
docs.text:
Headers are applied in a specific order: remove_headers first, then override_headers, then add_headers.

  • override_headers replaces all values for a given header key
  • add_headers appends values to existing headers (supports multi-value headers)
  • remove_headers removes headers by name
// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_headers": [
      {"key": "Content-Type", "value": "application/json"}
    ],
    "add_headers": [
      {"key": "X-Custom", "value": "value1"},
      {"key": "X-Custom", "value": "value2"}
    ],
    "remove_headers": ["X-Deprecated-Header"]
  }
}

docs.title: Body override
docs.text:
Replace the entire request body with text or Base64-encoded binary data:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_body": "{\"username\": \"admin\", \"role\": \"superuser\"}"
  }
}

For binary data, use override_body_base64:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_body_base64": "SGVsbG8gV29ybGQ="
  }
}

Mutual exclusivity

override_body and override_body_base64 are mutually exclusive. If either is set, body_patches are ignored.

docs.title: JSON patches
docs.text:
For surgical modifications to JSON bodies, use body_patches with json_path. The path uses simplified dot notation ($.key1.key2):

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "body_patches": [
      {"json_path": "$.user.role", "value": "admin"},
      {"json_path": "$.user.active", "value": true},
      {"json_path": "$.settings.limit", "value": 9999}
    ]
  }
}

JSON path limitations

The JSON path parser supports dot notation only ($.key1.key2.key3). Array index notation is not supported. The $ prefix is optional.

docs.title: Regex body patches
docs.text:
For text-based modifications, use body_patches with regex and replace. Capture group references ($1, $2) are supported:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "body_patches": [
      {"regex": "csrf_token=[^&]+", "replace": "csrf_token=injected_value"},
      {"regex": "(role=)(user)", "replace": "${1}admin"}
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Encoding chains
docs.text:
Both JSON and regex patches support an encoding array that applies codec transformations to the patch value before applying. Codecs are applied in pipeline order:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "body_patches": [
      {
        "json_path": "$.token",
        "value": "<script>alert(1)</script>",
        "encoding": ["url_encode_query", "base64"]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Available codecs: base64, base64url, url_encode_query, url_encode_path, url_encode_full, double_url_encode, hex, html_entity, html_escape, unicode_escape, md5, sha256, lower, upper.

Maximum chain length is 10 codecs.

docs.title: Body mutation priority
docs.text:
The body is resolved in this priority order:

  1. override_body or override_body_base64 (full replacement, highest priority)
  2. body_patches (partial modifications)
  3. Original body from the recorded flow

docs.title: Dry-run preview
docs.text:
Use dry_run: true to preview the modified request without sending it. This returns the final method, URL, headers, and body after all mutations are applied:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "override_method": "POST",
    "body_patches": [{"json_path": "$.user.role", "value": "admin"}],
    "dry_run": true
  }
}

The response includes a request_preview object with method, url, headers, body, and body_encoding.

docs.title: Raw HTTP resend
docs.text:
The resend_raw action sends the raw bytes from a recorded flow over a TCP/TLS connection. This is useful for testing HTTP request smuggling, malformed requests, or protocol-level vulnerabilities.

// resend
{
  "action": "resend_raw",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "target_addr": "target.com:443",
    "use_tls": true
  }
}

docs.title: Raw byte patches
docs.text:
You can modify raw bytes with three patch modes:

Offset overwrite -- overwrite bytes at a specific position:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend_raw",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "patches": [
      {"offset": 0, "data_base64": "R0VU"}
    ]
  }
}

Binary find and replace:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend_raw",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "patches": [
      {"find_base64": "SFRUUC8xLjE=", "replace_base64": "SFRUUC8xLjA="}
    ]
  }
}

Text find and replace:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend_raw",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "patches": [
      {"find_text": "Host: original.com", "replace_text": "Host: target.com"}
    ]
  }
}

You can also fully replace the raw bytes with override_raw_base64.

docs.title: TCP replay
docs.text:
The tcp_replay action replays a Raw TCP flow by sending all recorded send messages sequentially to the target. This records the entire exchange as a new TCP flow:

// resend
{
  "action": "tcp_replay",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "tcp-flow-456",
    "target_addr": "db.target.com:3306",
    "tag": "tcp-replay-test"
  }
}

docs.title: Multi-protocol support
docs.text:
The resender handles different protocols automatically:

Protocol Behavior HTTP/1.x Standard HTTP resend with full mutation support HTTPS Same as HTTP/1.x, with TLS HTTP/2 Resends using HTTP/1.1 fallback; all mutation options apply WebSocket Requires message_sequence to identify the message; sends as raw TCP frame Raw TCP Use tcp_replay action

docs.title: WebSocket resend
docs.text:
For WebSocket flows, specify the message_sequence to identify which message to resend:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "ws-flow-123",
    "message_sequence": 2,
    "target_addr": "ws.target.com:443",
    "use_tls": true
  }
}

docs.title: Additional options
docs.text: Parameter Type Description override_host string TCP connection target as host:port, independent of the URL host follow_redirects boolean Follow HTTP redirects automatically (default: false) timeout_ms integer Request timeout in milliseconds (default: 30000) tag string Tag to attach to the result flow for identification

docs.title: Hooks integration
docs.text:
The resender supports pre/post hooks that execute macros before sending and after receiving. See Macros for details on hook configuration.

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "hooks": {
      "pre_send": {
        "macro": "refresh-auth",
        "run_interval": "always"
      },
      "post_receive": {
        "macro": "log-response",
        "run_interval": "on_status",
        "status_codes": [401, 403]
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Authentication testing
docs.text:
Resend a login request with different credentials to test for weak password policies:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "login-flow-id",
    "body_patches": [
      {"json_path": "$.password", "value": "admin123"}
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Parameter tampering
docs.text:
Modify a recorded API request to test for authorization bypass:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "api-flow-id",
    "body_patches": [
      {"json_path": "$.user_id", "value": 1},
      {"json_path": "$.role", "value": "admin"}
    ],
    "tag": "idor-test"
  }
}

docs.title: HTTP request smuggling
docs.text:
Use raw resend with text patches to test for request smuggling:

// resend
{
  "action": "resend_raw",
  "params": {
    "flow_id": "abc-123",
    "patches": [
      {"find_text": "Content-Length: 42", "replace_text": "Transfer-Encoding: chunked"}
    ],
    "target_addr": "target.com:80"
  }
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Resend tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Fuzzer -- Automated fuzzing with payload injection
  • Comparer -- Compare responses from resend operations
  • Macros -- Hook integration for multi-step workflows

docs.title: SafetyFilter
docs.text: SafetyFilter is a two-part content filtering system that prevents destructive payloads from being sent to target systems (Input Filter) and protects sensitive information from being exposed to AI agents (Output Filter). SafetyFilter rules are part of the Policy layer and cannot be modified at runtime.

docs.title: Input filter
docs.text: The Input Filter inspects outgoing HTTP requests (body, URL, query string, headers) against regex rules and blocks or logs matches before the request reaches the target.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
When a request is about to be sent (via resend, fuzz, macro, or intercept modify_and_forward), the Input Filter:

  1. Evaluates the request body, URL, query string, and headers against all input rules
  2. If a rule matches with block action, the request is rejected with an error
  3. If a rule matches with log_only action, the match is logged but the request proceeds

docs.title: Built-in presets
docs.text: Preset Rules Description destructive-sql 6 rules DROP TABLE/DATABASE/INDEX/VIEW/SCHEMA, TRUNCATE TABLE, DELETE without WHERE, UPDATE WHERE 1=1, ALTER TABLE DROP, xp_ stored procedures destructive-os-command 5 rules rm -rf, shutdown/reboot/halt/poweroff, mkfs, dd if=, Windows format

docs.title: Input targets
docs.text: Target Description body Request body content url Full URL string query Query string portion of the URL header Individual header values (use header:Name for specific headers) headers All header values concatenated

docs.title: Blocked response
docs.text:
When the Input Filter blocks a request at the proxy layer:

  • Status: 403 Forbidden
  • Header: X-Block-Reason: safety_filter
  • Body: JSON with violation details (rule ID, rule name, match location)

When it blocks an MCP tool operation:

  • MCP error response with violation details

docs.title: Output filter
docs.text: The Output Filter prevents sensitive information (PII) from being exposed to AI agents. It inspects response bodies and headers against regex rules and masks matching content before returning data to the agent.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Proxy layer: Response body and headers are masked before returning to the client
  2. MCP tool layer: Query results, resend responses, fuzz results, intercept queue entries, compare diffs, and export data are masked before returning to the AI agent
  3. Raw data preserved: The Flow Store always contains the original unmasked data for human review via the Web UI

docs.title: Built-in PII presets
docs.text: Preset Rules Description credit-card 2 rules Credit card numbers in separated (1234-5678-9012-3456) and continuous (1234567890123456) formats japan-my-number 1 rule Japanese My Number (12-digit individual number) with check digit validation email 1 rule Email addresses (user@example.com) japan-phone 2 rules Japanese phone numbers in mobile (090-1234-5678) and landline (03-1234-5678) formats

docs.title: Validators
docs.text:
Some presets use validator functions for additional verification beyond regex matching, reducing false positives:

  • credit-card (continuous): Luhn algorithm check -- only masks digit sequences that pass the Luhn checksum
  • japan-my-number: Check digit validation -- only masks 12-digit sequences with a valid My Number check digit

docs.title: Output actions
docs.text: Action Description mask Replace matched content with the replacement string log_only Log the match but return data unmodified

docs.title: Replacement strings
docs.text:
Replacement strings support regex capture group references:

Syntax Description [MASKED:credit_card] Static replacement $1 First capture group ${name} Named capture group

docs.title: Two-layer architecture
docs.text:
Like Target scope and Rate limits, SafetyFilter uses the Policy + Agent two-layer architecture:

  • Policy layer: SafetyFilter rules are defined in the config file and are immutable at runtime
  • Agent layer: AI agents can view rules via get_safety_filter but cannot modify them

This ensures that safety boundaries remain enforced regardless of agent behavior.

docs.title: Enforcement points
docs.text:
The Input Filter is enforced at every point where the proxy sends data to external targets:

Tool Enforcement resend Body, URL, and headers checked before sending resend_raw Raw bytes checked before sending fuzz Template flow checked at start; each expanded payload checked before sending macro Each step's outbound request checked before sending intercept modify_and_forward body, URL, and headers checked before forwarding

The Output Filter is enforced at every point where data is returned to the AI agent:

Source Enforcement Proxy responses Body and headers masked before returning to client query results Flow data masked in query output resend results Response body and headers masked fuzz results Response data masked compare diffs Header diff values masked export data Inline export data masked

docs.title: Viewing current rules
docs.text:
Use the security tool to view the current SafetyFilter configuration:

// security
{
  "action": "get_safety_filter"
}

Response:

{
  "enabled": true,
  "input_rules": [
    {
      "id": "destructive-sql:drop",
      "name": "DROP statement",
      "pattern": "(compiled regex)",
      "targets": ["body", "url", "query"],
      "action": "block",
      "category": "destructive-sql"
    }
  ],
  "output_rules": [
    {
      "id": "credit-card:separated",
      "name": "Credit card (separated)",
      "pattern": "(compiled regex)",
      "targets": ["body"],
      "action": "mask",
      "replacement": "[MASKED:credit_card]",
      "category": "credit-card"
    }
  ],
  "immutable": true
}

docs.title: Custom rules
docs.text: Custom rules are defined in the config file.

docs.title: Custom input rule
docs.text:
{
  "id": "custom-api",
  "name": "Dangerous API endpoint",
  "pattern": "(?i)/api/v[0-9]+/(delete-all|reset)",
  "targets": ["url"]
}

docs.title: Custom output rule
docs.text:
{
  "id": "custom-api-key",
  "name": "API key pattern",
  "pattern": "(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{32,})",
  "targets": ["body"],
  "action": "mask",
  "replacement": "[MASKED:api_key]"
}

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Security tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Target scope -- Host-level access control
  • Rate limits & budgets -- Rate limiting and budget controls
  • Security model -- Architecture-level security design
  • Config file -- SafetyFilter configuration

docs.title: Target scope
docs.text: Target scope controls which hosts and URLs the proxy is allowed to send requests to. It uses a two-layer architecture that separates policy-level controls (set by the operator) from agent-level controls (set by the AI agent at runtime).

docs.title: Policy layer
docs.text: The Policy layer is set at startup from the configuration file and cannot be modified at runtime. It defines the upper boundary for what the agent can access.

docs.title: Agent layer
docs.text: The Agent layer is controlled by the AI agent via the security tool. It can further restrict access within the Policy layer boundaries, but cannot expand beyond them.

docs.title: Design rationale
docs.text:
This separation ensures that:

  • Operators define the maximum scope of testing (Policy layer)
  • AI agents can self-restrict within that scope (Agent layer)
  • Safety is enforced even if the agent attempts to bypass restrictions

docs.title: Evaluation order
docs.text:
When a request URL is checked against target scope:

  1. Policy denies -- always block (highest priority)
  2. Agent denies -- block
  3. Policy allows (if any) -- target must match at least one, otherwise block
  4. Agent allows (if any) -- target must match at least one, otherwise block
  5. All checks passed -- allow

The priority order is: deny > allow, and within each type: policy > agent.

When neither layer has rules, all targets are permitted (open mode).

docs.title: Rule definition
docs.text:
Each target rule specifies:

Field Type Description hostname string Exact match or wildcard *.example.com (required) ports array of int Match specific ports (empty = all ports) path_prefix string Match URL path prefix (empty = all paths) schemes array of string Match URL schemes: http, https (empty = all schemes)

All specified fields must match for a rule to apply (AND logic).

docs.title: Wildcard hostnames
docs.text:
Use *.example.com to match subdomains. This matches sub.example.com but not example.com itself:

{"hostname": "*.target.com"}

docs.title: Config file policy setup
docs.text:
Set Policy layer rules in the config file:

{
  "target_scope": {
    "allows": [
      {"hostname": "*.target.com"},
      {"hostname": "api.partner.com", "ports": [443], "schemes": ["https"]}
    ],
    "denies": [
      {"hostname": "*.internal.corp"},
      {"hostname": "admin.target.com"}
    ]
  }
}

Policy rules are loaded at startup and are immutable.

docs.title: Set agent scope (full replace)
docs.text:
Replace all Agent layer rules:

// security
{
  "action": "set_target_scope",
  "params": {
    "allows": [
      {"hostname": "api.target.com", "ports": [443], "schemes": ["https"]},
      {"hostname": "*.target.com"}
    ],
    "denies": [
      {"hostname": "admin.target.com"}
    ]
  }
}

Use empty arrays to clear all agent rules:

// security
{
  "action": "set_target_scope",
  "params": {
    "allows": [],
    "denies": []
  }
}

Policy boundary enforcement

Agent allow rules must fall within the Policy allow boundary. If any agent allow rule is outside the policy scope, the entire operation is rejected.

docs.title: Update agent scope (incremental)
docs.text:
Apply incremental changes to Agent layer rules:

// security
{
  "action": "update_target_scope",
  "params": {
    "add_allows": [{"hostname": "new-api.target.com"}],
    "remove_allows": [{"hostname": "old-api.target.com"}],
    "add_denies": [{"hostname": "staging.target.com"}]
  }
}

Policy deny protection

You cannot remove Policy deny rules via remove_denies. Attempting to do so returns an error because policy rules are immutable.

docs.title: View current scope
docs.text:
// security
{
  "action": "get_target_scope"
}

Returns both Policy and Agent layer rules with the enforcement mode:

{
  "policy": {
    "allows": [{"hostname": "*.target.com"}],
    "denies": [{"hostname": "*.internal.corp"}],
    "source": "config file",
    "immutable": true
  },
  "agent": {
    "allows": [{"hostname": "api.target.com"}],
    "denies": [{"hostname": "admin.target.com"}]
  },
  "effective_mode": "enforcing"
}

docs.title: URL test
docs.text:
Test a URL against current rules without making a request:

// security
{
  "action": "test_target",
  "params": {
    "url": "https://api.target.com/v1/users"
  }
}

The response tells you:

  • Whether the URL is allowed
  • Which layer made the decision
  • Which rule matched
  • The parsed URL components that were evaluated
{
  "allowed": true,
  "reason": "",
  "layer": "agent",
  "matched_rule": {"hostname": "api.target.com"},
  "tested_target": {
    "hostname": "api.target.com",
    "port": 443,
    "scheme": "https",
    "path": "/v1/users"
  }
}

docs.title: Enforcement modes
docs.text: Mode Description open No rules configured in either layer; all targets permitted enforcing At least one rule exists; targets are checked against rules

docs.title: Where target scope is enforced
docs.text:
Target scope is checked at multiple points:

  • Resend -- before sending the request and after URL override
  • Resend raw -- before connecting to the target address
  • Fuzz -- on the template flow URL and after payload injection
  • Macro -- on each step's URL before execution and at send time after template expansion
  • Intercept -- on modify_and_forward URL overrides

This multi-point enforcement prevents SSRF via payload injection or template expansion.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Security tool reference -- MCP tool parameter reference
  • Security model -- Architecture-level security design
  • SafetyFilter -- Input/output content filtering
  • Rate limits & budgets -- Rate limiting and budget controls

docs.title: Technology detection
docs.text: yorishiro-proxy automatically detects the technology stack of target applications by analyzing HTTP response headers, cookies, and body content. Detection results are stored as flow tags and aggregated per host for easy querying.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
When HTTP responses pass through the proxy, the fingerprinting engine analyzes:

  • HTTP response headers (e.g. Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version)
  • Cookies (e.g. PHPSESSID, JSESSIONID, csrftoken)
  • Response body content (e.g. generator meta tags, framework-specific patterns)

Detection results are stored in each flow's tags under the technologies key as a JSON array.

docs.title: Detection categories
docs.text:
The fingerprinting engine detects technologies across several categories:

Category Examples Framework Django, Rails, Express, Laravel, Spring Language PHP, Python, Java, Ruby, ASP.NET Server nginx, Apache, IIS, Cloudflare CMS WordPress, Drupal, Joomla CDN Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Akamai Security WAF signatures, CSP configurations

docs.title: Querying detected technologies
docs.text:
Use the query tool to retrieve aggregated technology detection results per host:

// query
{
  "action": "technologies"
}

The response groups detected technologies by host:

{
  "hosts": [
    {
      "host": "api.target.com",
      "technologies": [
        {
          "name": "nginx",
          "version": "1.24.0",
          "category": "server",
          "confidence": "high"
        },
        {
          "name": "Django",
          "version": "",
          "category": "framework",
          "confidence": "medium"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "host": "www.target.com",
      "technologies": [
        {
          "name": "Apache",
          "version": "2.4",
          "category": "server",
          "confidence": "high"
        },
        {
          "name": "PHP",
          "version": "8.2",
          "category": "language",
          "confidence": "high"
        },
        {
          "name": "WordPress",
          "version": "6.4",
          "category": "cms",
          "confidence": "high"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "count": 2
}

docs.title: Detection details
docs.text:
Each detection entry includes:

Field Description name Technology name version Detected version (empty if unknown) category Technology category (server, framework, language, etc.) confidence Detection confidence level

docs.title: Confidence levels
docs.text:
Detection results have a confidence level based on the signal strength:

  • high -- detected from explicit headers (e.g. Server: nginx/1.24.0) or known unique indicators
  • medium -- detected from indirect signals (e.g. default cookie names, common patterns)
  • low -- detected from heuristic analysis

docs.title: Version detection
docs.text:
When version information is available in headers or response content, it is extracted and included. For example:

  • Server: nginx/1.24.0 produces version "1.24.0"
  • X-Powered-By: PHP/8.2.0 produces version "8.2.0"

If the version cannot be determined, the version field is empty.

docs.title: Deduplication
docs.text: Technologies are deduplicated per host by name and category. When multiple flows for the same host detect the same technology, the entry with version information is preferred over one without.

docs.title: Reconnaissance
docs.text:
Technology detection provides automatic fingerprinting during the reconnaissance phase. After browsing a target application through the proxy, query the detected technologies to understand the stack:

// query
{
  "action": "technologies"
}

docs.title: Targeted vulnerability testing
docs.text: Use detection results to focus testing on known vulnerabilities for the detected stack. For example, if WordPress is detected, prioritize WordPress-specific vulnerability checks.

docs.title: Change detection
docs.text: Compare technology detection results across different hosts or time periods to identify infrastructure changes or inconsistencies in a target's environment.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Query tool reference -- Technologies query action
  • Flows -- Flow tags and metadata

docs.title: CA certificate
docs.text: yorishiro-proxy performs HTTPS interception (MITM) by dynamically generating server certificates signed by its own CA. To inspect HTTPS traffic, you must install this CA certificate in your operating system or browser trust store.

docs.title: Automatic generation
docs.text:
On first startup, the CA certificate is automatically generated and saved to:

~/.yorishiro-proxy/ca/ca.crt   (certificate)
~/.yorishiro-proxy/ca/ca.key   (private key)

No manual action is required to generate the CA -- it is created the first time yorishiro-proxy runs.

Tip

Running yorishiro-proxy install ca --trust generates the CA certificate and registers it in your OS trust store automatically. You can skip the manual steps below.

docs.title: Check the CA certificate path
docs.text:
Use the query tool to confirm the CA certificate location:

// query
{"resource": "ca_cert"}

The response includes cert_path (file path) and fingerprint (SHA-256 hash) fields.

docs.title: OS-specific installation
docs.text:
macOSLinuxWindows 
sudo security add-trusted-cert -d -r trustRoot \
  -k /Library/Keychains/System.keychain \
  ~/.yorishiro-proxy/ca/ca.crt

sudo cp ~/.yorishiro-proxy/ca/ca.crt \
  /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/yorishiro-proxy.crt
sudo update-ca-certificates

Run the following command in an Administrator command prompt:

certutil -addstore "Root" %USERPROFILE%\.yorishiro-proxy\ca\ca.crt

docs.title: Alternative: skip CA installation with playwright-cli
docs.text:
If you are using playwright-cli for browser automation, you can skip CA certificate installation by enabling ignoreHTTPSErrors in the playwright configuration. This tells the browser to accept any certificate without verification.

Create .playwright/cli.config.json in your project root:

{
  "browser": {
    "browserName": "chromium",
    "launchOptions": {
      "channel": "chromium",
      "proxy": {
        "server": "http://127.0.0.1:8080"
      }
    },
    "contextOptions": {
      "ignoreHTTPSErrors": true
    }
  }
}

With this configuration, playwright-cli will route traffic through the proxy and accept yorishiro-proxy's dynamically generated certificates without needing to install the CA in the OS trust store.

docs.title: Ephemeral CA
docs.text:
